chapter.title: Respiration in Plants
chapter.summary:
This chapter explores cellular respiration in plants, the process by which plants break down organic molecules to release energy in the form of ATP. It begins by emphasizing the necessity of energy for life processes and the autotrophic nature of plants, highlighting photosynthesis as the foundation for energy storage. Heterotrophic organisms obtain their energy from these stored compounds. Cellular respiration is defined as the controlled oxidation of respiratory substrates like glucose, commonly derived from sucrose or stored carbohydrates, to release energy.

The chapter addresses how plants, unlike animals, manage gaseous exchange via stomata and lenticels due to their unique structure and lower metabolic rate. Glycolysis, the initial stage, occurs in the cytoplasm and converts glucose into pyruvate. The fate of pyruvate depends on oxygen availability, leading to fermentation (anaerobic) or aerobic respiration. Fermentation yields minimal ATP, while aerobic respiration, occurring in mitochondria, involves the Krebs cycle (TCA cycle) and the electron transport system (ETS).

The ETS generates a proton gradient, driving ATP synthesis through oxidative phosphorylation. The chapter details the theoretical ATP yield from glucose oxidation, though acknowledges that real-world conditions vary. It presents the concept of the respiratory pathway as amphibolic, serving both catabolic and anabolic roles. Finally, the respiratory quotient (RQ), the ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed, indicates the type of substrate being respired, with values differing for carbohydrates, fats, and proteins. Understanding respiration is crucial for comprehending plant metabolism, growth, and adaptation to various environments.

subsections.title: Energy Requirements of Living Organisms
subsections.content_text: All living organisms require energy to perform essential life activities such as absorption, transport, movement, reproduction, and even breathing. This energy is derived from the food we eat. While the process of breathing is distinct, it is fundamentally connected to the release of energy from food. The energy required for all 'life' processes is obtained through the oxidation of macromolecules, collectively referred to as 'food'.

subsections.title: Photosynthesis and Energy Storage
subsections.content_text: Green plants and cyanobacteria are autotrophic, meaning they prepare their own food. Through photosynthesis, they trap light energy and convert it into chemical energy, which is stored within the bonds of carbohydrates like glucose, sucrose, and starch. It is important to note that even in green plants, only cells containing chloroplasts (mostly in superficial layers) can photosynthesize. Therefore, non-green organs, tissues, and cells require the translocation of food for their oxidation needs.

subsections.title: Heterotrophic Nutrition
subsections.content_text: Animals are heterotrophic, obtaining their food either directly from plants (herbivores) or indirectly (carnivores). Saprophytes, such as fungi, are dependent on dead and decaying organic matter. Ultimately, all the food respired for life processes originates from photosynthesis.

subsections.title: Definition of Cellular Respiration
subsections.content_text: Cellular respiration is the mechanism of breaking down food materials within the cell to release energy, and the subsequent trapping of this energy for the synthesis of ATP. While photosynthesis occurs within chloroplasts in eukaryotes, the breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy occurs in the cytoplasm and the mitochondria.

subsections.title: Respiratory Substrates and Energy Release
subsections.content_text: Respiration is defined as the breaking of the C-C bonds of complex compounds through oxidation within the cells, leading to the release of a considerable amount of energy. The compounds that are oxidized during this process are known as respiratory substrates. While carbohydrates are the most common substrates, proteins, fats, and organic acids can also be used under certain conditions. The energy contained in these substrates is not released in a single step; instead, it is released in a series of slow, step-wise reactions controlled by enzymes.

subsections.title: ATP: The Energy Currency
subsections.content_text: The energy released during oxidation is trapped as chemical energy in the form of Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP). ATP acts as the energy currency of the cell, being broken down whenever and wherever energy is needed for various energy-requiring processes. Additionally, the carbon skeletons produced during respiration serve as precursors for the biosynthesis of other molecules within the cell.

subsections.title: Gaseous Exchange in Plants
subsections.content_text: Plants require $O_2$ for respiration and release $CO_2$. Unlike animals, plants do not have specialized organs for gaseous exchange. Instead, they utilize stomata and lenticels for this purpose.

subsections.title: Reasons for the Absence of Respiratory Organs
subsections.content_text: There are several reasons why plants can function without specialized respiratory organs: 1. Each plant part takes care of its own gas-exchange needs, resulting in very little transport of gases between parts. 2. Plants have low demands for gas exchange; roots, stems, and leaves respire at rates far lower than animals. Large volumes of gas are only exchanged during photosynthesis, where $O_2$ is released internally within the cells. 3. The distance for gas diffusion is short, even in large plants. Living cells are located close to the surface, and in stems, they are organized in thin layers beneath the bark (accessible via lenticels).

subsections.title: Structural Adaptations for Diffusion
subsections.content_text: In addition to being close to the surface, the interior cells of bulky plant organs are often dead and provide only mechanical support. The loose packing of parenchyma cells in leaves, stems, and roots provides an interconnected network of air spaces that facilitates the diffusion of gases.

subsections.title: The Chemical Equation of Respiration
subsections.content_text: The complete combustion of glucose yields $CO_2$, $H_2O$, and energy. The overall reaction is represented as: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + Energy$$ To be useful, the cell must utilize this energy to synthesize other molecules (ATP) rather than losing it all as heat. This is achieved by catabolizing the glucose molecule in small, enzyme-controlled steps.

subsections.title: Anaerobic Respiration and Evolution
subsections.content_text: Respiration can occur in the absence of oxygen. It is believed that the first cells on Earth lived in an atmosphere lacking oxygen. Even today, many organisms are adapted to anaerobic conditions. Some are facultative anaerobes, while others are obligate anaerobes. All living organisms retain the enzymatic machinery to partially oxidize glucose without oxygen, a process called glycolysis.

subsections.title: Etymology and the EMP Pathway
subsections.content_text: The term glycolysis originates from the Greek words 'glycos' for sugar and 'lysis' for splitting. The scheme of glycolysis was elucidated by Gustav Embden, Otto Meyerhof, and J. Parnas, and is frequently referred to as the EMP pathway.

subsections.title: Occurrence and Initial Substrates
subsections.content_text: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell and is present in all living organisms. In this process, glucose undergoes partial oxidation to form two molecules of pyruvic acid. In plants, this glucose is typically derived from sucrose (the end product of photosynthesis) or from storage carbohydrates. In plants, glucose is typically derived from sucrose (the end product of photosynthesis) or from storage carbohydrates. Sucrose is converted into glucose and fructose by the enzyme, invertase, and these two monosaccharides readily enter the glycolytic pathway. Glucose and fructose are phosphorylated to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate by the activity of the enzyme hexokinase. This phosphorylated form of glucose then isomerises to produce fructose-6-phosphate. Subsequent steps of metabolism of glucose and fructose are the same.

subsections.title: The Ten Steps of Glycolysis
subsections.content_text: Glycolysis consists of a chain of ten reactions, under the control of different enzymes, which take place to produce pyruvate from glucose. The various steps are depicted in Figure 12.1. During these steps, it is important to track where the utilisation or synthesis of ATP and $NADH + H^+$ occurs.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.1
diagrams.description: A flow chart showing the ten steps of glycolysis: Glucose (6C) is converted to Glucose-6-phosphate (6C) using ATP; then to Fructose-6-phosphate (6C); then to Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate (6C) using another ATP. This is split into two Triose phosphates: Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (3C) and Dihydroxy acetone phosphate (3C). Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate is converted to 1,3-bisphosphoglyceric acid (BPGA) while $NAD^+$ is reduced to $NADH + H^+$. BPGA is converted to 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA) generating ATP. PGA is converted to 2-phosphoglycerate, then to phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) with the release of $H_2O$, and finally to Pyruvic acid (3C) generating another ATP.

subsections.title: ATP Consumption and Molecular Splitting
subsections.content_text: ATP is utilised at two specific steps in glycolysis: first, in the conversion of glucose into glucose 6-phosphate, and second, in the conversion of fructose 6-phosphate to fructose 1, 6-bisphosphate. The fructose 1, 6-bisphosphate is then split into two three-carbon molecules: dihydroxyacetone phosphate and 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde (PGAL).

subsections.title: Redox Reactions and ATP Generation
subsections.content_text: There is one step where $NADH + H^+$ is formed from $NAD^+$; this occurs when 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde (PGAL) is converted to 1, 3-bisphosphoglycerate (BPGA). During this step, two redox-equivalents (in the form of two hydrogen atoms) are removed from PGAL and transferred to a molecule of $NAD^+$. PGAL is oxidised and, with inorganic phosphate, is converted into BPGA. The conversion of BPGA to 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA) is an energy-yielding process where the energy is trapped by the formation of ATP. Another ATP is synthesised during the final conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) to pyruvic acid.

subsections.title: Summary and Metabolic Fate of Pyruvate
subsections.content_text: Pyruvic acid is the key product of glycolysis. Its metabolic fate depends on the cellular needs and the availability of oxygen. There are three major ways in which different cells handle pyruvic acid: lactic acid fermentation, alcoholic fermentation, and aerobic respiration. Fermentation occurs under anaerobic conditions in many prokaryotes and unicellular eukaryotes. For complete oxidation to $CO_2$ and $H_2O$, organisms adopt the Krebs' cycle (aerobic respiration), which requires an $O_2$ supply.

subsections.title: Overview of Anaerobic Pathways
subsections.content_text: In fermentation, such as in yeast, the incomplete oxidation of glucose is achieved under anaerobic conditions. Pyruvic acid is converted to $CO_2$ and ethanol. This process is catalysed by the enzymes pyruvic acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase. Other organisms, like certain bacteria, produce lactic acid from pyruvic acid. In animal cells, such as muscles during exercise when oxygen is inadequate, pyruvic acid is reduced to lactic acid by lactate dehydrogenase. In both processes, the reducing agent is $NADH + H^+$, which is reoxidised to $NAD^+$.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.2
diagrams.description: Diagram showing major pathways of anaerobic respiration. Glucose is converted to Glyceraldehyde 3-Phosphate, then to 3-Phosphoglyceric acid (generating $NADH + H^+$), then to Phosphoenol Pyruvic acid, and finally to Pyruvic acid. From Pyruvic acid, two pathways are shown: one leading to Lactic acid and the other leading to Ethanol + $CO_2$. Both pathways involve the reoxidation of $NADH + H^+$ to $NAD^+$.

subsections.title: Energy Yield and Toxicity in Fermentation
subsections.content_text: In both lactic acid and alcohol fermentation, the energy release is minimal; less than seven per cent of the energy in glucose is released, and not all of it is trapped in the high-energy bonds of ATP. These processes can be hazardous; for instance, yeasts poison themselves to death when the concentration of alcohol reaches about 13 per cent. This limits the maximum concentration of alcohol in naturally fermented beverages.

subsections.title: Mechanism and Location of Aerobic Respiration
subsections.content_text: Aerobic respiration is the process that leads to a complete oxidation of organic substances in the presence of oxygen, releasing $CO_2$, water, and a large amount of energy. In eukaryotes, these steps take place within the mitochondria. The crucial events are: 1. The complete oxidation of pyruvate by the stepwise removal of all the hydrogen atoms, leaving three molecules of $CO_2$. 2. The passing on of the electrons removed as part of the hydrogen atoms to molecular $O_2$ with simultaneous synthesis of ATP. The first process occurs in the matrix of the mitochondria, while the second is located on the inner membrane.

subsections.title: Oxidative Decarboxylation of Pyruvate
subsections.content_text: Pyruvate, formed in the cytosol by glycolysis, enters the mitochondrial matrix and undergoes oxidative decarboxylation. This reaction is catalysed by a complex set of reactions involving the enzyme pyruvate dehydrogenase and requires several coenzymes, including $NAD^+$ and Coenzyme A. The reaction is: $$\text{Pyruvic acid} + 	ext{CoA} + 	ext{NAD}^+ \xrightarrow[\text{Pyruvate dehydrogenase}]{\text{Mg}^{2+}} \text{Acetyl CoA} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+$$ During this process, two molecules of $NADH$ are produced from the metabolism of two molecules of pyruvic acid (derived from one glucose molecule). The resulting Acetyl CoA then enters the tricarboxylic acid cycle.

subsections.title: Initial Steps of the TCA Cycle
subsections.content_text: The TCA cycle (also known as the Krebs' cycle) starts with the condensation of the acetyl group with oxaloacetic acid (OAA) and water to yield citric acid. This reaction is catalysed by the enzyme citrate synthase, and a molecule of CoA is released. Citrate is then isomerised to isocitrate. This is followed by two successive steps of decarboxylation, leading to the formation of $\alpha$-ketoglutaric acid and then succinyl-CoA. In the remaining steps of citric acid cycle, succinyl-CoA is oxidised to OAA allowing the cycle to continue. During the conversion of succinyl-CoA to succinic acid a molecule of GTP is synthesised. This is a substrate level phosphorylation. In a coupled reaction GTP is converted to GDP with the simultaneous synthesis of ATP from ADP.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.3
diagrams.description: A cyclic diagram of the Citric acid cycle (Krebs Cycle) showing the conversion of Pyruvate (3C) to Acetyl coenzyme A (2C), which enters the cycle by reacting with Oxaloacetic acid (4C) to form Citric acid (6C). The cycle proceeds through $\alpha$-ketoglutaric acid (5C), Succinic acid (4C), and Malic acid (4C). It highlights the release of $CO_2$, the reduction of $NAD^+$ to $NADH+H^+$ at three points, the reduction of $FAD^+$ to $FADH_2$, and the synthesis of GTP.

subsections.title: Regeneration of Co-factors and OAA
subsections.content_text: There are three points in the cycle where $NAD^+$ is reduced to $NADH + H^+$ and one point where $FAD^+$ is reduced to $FADH_2$. The continued oxidation of acetyl CoA via the TCA cycle requires the continued replenishment of oxaloacetic acid (OAA), the first member of the cycle. In addition, it also requires regeneration of $NAD^+$ and $FAD^+$ from $NADH$ and $FADH_2$ respectively.

subsections.title: Summary Equation of the TCA Cycle
subsections.content_text: The summary equation for this phase of respiration, representing the oxidation of pyruvic acid in the mitochondrial matrix, is written as: $$Pyruvic acid + 4NAD^+ + FAD^+ + 2H_2O + ADP + Pi \xrightarrow{Mitochondrial Matrix} 3CO_2 + 4NADH + 4H^+ + FADH_2 + ATP$$ Till now, glucose has been broken down to release $CO_2$, and eight molecules of $NADH + H^+$ and two of $FADH_2$ have been synthesised (including the link reaction), besides just two molecules of ATP in the TCA cycle.

sections.title: Electron Transport System (ETS) and Oxidative Phosphorylation

subsections.title: Introduction to the Electron Transport System
subsections.content_text: The following steps in the respiratory process are to release and utilise the energy stored in $NADH+H^+$ and $FADH_2$. This is accomplished when they are oxidised through the electron transport system (ETS) and the electrons are passed on to $O_2$ resulting in the formation of $H_2O$. The metabolic pathway through which the electron passes from one carrier to another is called the electron transport system (ETS) and it is present in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

subsections.title: Complex I and Ubiquinone
subsections.content_text: Electrons from NADH produced in the mitochondrial matrix during the citric acid cycle are oxidised by an NADH dehydrogenase (complex I), and electrons are then transferred to ubiquinone located within the inner membrane. Ubiquinone also receives reducing equivalents via $FADH_2$ (complex II) that is generated during oxidation of succinate in the citric acid cycle.

subsections.title: Complex III and Cytochrome c
subsections.content_text: The reduced ubiquinone (ubiquinol) is then oxidised with the transfer of electrons to cytochrome $c$ via cytochrome $bc_1$ complex (complex III). Cytochrome $c$ is a small protein attached to the outer surface of the inner membrane and acts as a mobile carrier for transfer of electrons between complex III and IV.

subsections.title: Complex IV and Terminal Oxidation
subsections.content_text: Complex IV refers to cytochrome $c$ oxidase complex containing cytochromes $a$ and $a_3$, and two copper centres. When the electrons pass from one carrier to another via complex I to IV in the electron transport chain, they are coupled to ATP synthase (complex V) for the production of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate. The number of ATP molecules synthesised depends on the nature of the electron donor. Oxidation of one molecule of NADH gives rise to 3 molecules of ATP, while that of one molecule of $FADH_2$ produces 2 molecules of ATP.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.4
diagrams.description: A detailed diagram of the Electron Transport System (ETS) located in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It shows the flow of electrons through Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), Complex II (Succinate dehydrogenase), Complex III (Cytochrome $bc_1$), and Complex IV (Cytochrome $c$ oxidase). It illustrates the pumping of protons ($H^+$) into the inter-membrane space, the role of ubiquinone (UQ) and cytochrome $c$ as mobile carriers, and the final reduction of oxygen to water. Complex V (ATP synthase) is shown utilizing the electrochemical gradient to produce ATP.

subsections.title: Role of Oxygen and Oxidative Phosphorylation
subsections.content_text: Although the aerobic process of respiration takes place only in the presence of oxygen, the role of oxygen is limited to the terminal stage of the process. Yet, the presence of oxygen is vital, since it drives the whole process by removing hydrogen from the system. Oxygen acts as the final hydrogen acceptor. Unlike photophosphorylation where light energy is utilised for the production of proton gradient, in respiration it is the energy of oxidation-reduction utilised for the same process. It is for this reason that the process is called oxidative phosphorylation.

subsections.title: Mechanism of ATP Synthesis (Complex V)
subsections.content_text: ATP synthesis is explained by the chemiosmotic hypothesis. The energy released during the ETS is utilised in synthesising ATP with the help of ATP synthase (complex V). This complex consists of two major components, $F_1$ and $F_0$. The $F_1$ headpiece is a peripheral membrane protein complex and contains the site for synthesis of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate. $F_0$ is an integral membrane protein complex that forms the channel through which protons cross the inner membrane. The passage of protons through the channel is coupled to the catalytic site of the $F_1$ component for the production of ATP. For each ATP produced, $4H^+$ pass through $F_0$ from the intermembrane space to the matrix down the electrochemical proton gradient.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.5
diagrams.description: Diagrammatic presentation of ATP synthesis in mitochondria. It shows the $F_0$ part embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane and the $F_1$ headpiece protruding into the matrix. $4H^+$ ions are shown moving from the intermembrane space (outer side) through the $F_0$ channel into the matrix, triggering the synthesis of ATP from $ADP + Pi$ at the $F_1$ component.

subsections.title: Assumptions for Calculating ATP Yield
subsections.content_text: Calculations of the net gain of ATP for every glucose molecule oxidised are based on several assumptions: 1. There is a sequential, orderly pathway functioning (Glycolysis, TCA cycle, and ETS). 2. NADH synthesised in glycolysis is transferred into the mitochondria for oxidative phosphorylation. 3. None of the intermediates in the pathway are utilised to synthesise any other compound. 4. Only glucose is being respired—no other alternative substrates enter the pathway at intermediary stages.

subsections.title: Net ATP Gain in Aerobic Respiration
subsections.content_text: In living systems, these assumptions are not strictly valid as pathways work simultaneously, substrates are withdrawn as needed, and enzymatic rates are controlled by multiple means. However, these calculations are useful to appreciate the efficiency of the system. Based on these assumptions, there can be a net gain of 38 ATP molecules during aerobic respiration of one molecule of glucose.

subsections.title: Comparison of Fermentation and Aerobic Respiration
subsections.content_text: A comparison between fermentation and aerobic respiration reveals several key differences. Firstly, fermentation accounts for only a partial breakdown of glucose, whereas in aerobic respiration, it is completely degraded to $CO_2$ and $H_2O$. Secondly, in fermentation, there is a net gain of only two molecules of ATP for each molecule of glucose degraded to pyruvic acid, whereas many more molecules of ATP are generated under aerobic conditions. Thirdly, NADH is oxidised to $NAD^+$ rather slowly in fermentation, however, the reaction is very vigorous in the case of aerobic respiration.

subsections.title: Entry Points of Different Substrates in the Respiratory Pathway
subsections.content_text: Glucose is the favoured substrate for respiration, and all carbohydrates are usually first converted into glucose before they are used. Other substrates can also be respired, but they do not enter the respiratory pathway at the first step. Fats are broken down into glycerol and fatty acids first; fatty acids are degraded to acetyl CoA to enter the pathway, while glycerol enters after being converted to PGAL. Proteins are degraded by proteases into individual amino acids which, after deamination, enter the pathway at some stage within the Krebs' cycle or even as pyruvate or acetyl CoA, depending on their structure. Figure 12.6 illustrates these interrelationships.

diagrams.label: Figure 12.6
diagrams.description: A flow chart showing the interrelationship among metabolic pathways for the breakdown of fats, carbohydrates, and proteins. Fats are shown breaking into fatty acids (entering at Acetyl CoA) and glycerol (entering at Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate). Carbohydrates enter as simple sugars like glucose, proceeding through glycolysis (Glucose 6-phosphate, Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate, DHAP, and Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate) to Pyruvic acid and then Acetyl CoA. Proteins break into amino acids which enter at Pyruvic acid, Acetyl CoA, or directly into the Krebs' cycle. The Krebs' cycle is shown releasing $H_2O$ and $CO_2$.

subsections.title: Respiration as an Amphibolic Pathway
subsections.content_text: Respiration has traditionally been considered a catabolic process because it involves the breakdown of substrates. However, the respiratory pathway is also involved in anabolism (synthesis). For example, when an organism needs to synthesise fatty acids, acetyl CoA is withdrawn from the respiratory pathway. Similarly, respiratory intermediates form the link during both the breakdown and synthesis of proteins. Because the respiratory pathway is involved in both catabolism and anabolism, it is better to consider it as an amphibolic pathway rather than a purely catabolic one.

subsections.title: Definition of Respiratory Quotient (RQ)
subsections.content_text: During aerobic respiration, $O_2$ is consumed and $CO_2$ is released. The ratio of the volume of $CO_2$ evolved to the volume of $O_2$ consumed in respiration is called the respiratory quotient (RQ) or respiratory ratio. The formula is: $RQ = \frac{\text{volume of } CO_2 \text{ evolved}}{\text{volume of } O_2 \text{ consumed}}$. The RQ depends upon the type of respiratory substrate used.

subsections.title: RQ for Carbohydrates
subsections.content_text: When carbohydrates are used as substrate and are completely oxidised, the RQ will be 1, because equal amounts of $CO_2$ and $O_2$ are evolved and consumed, as shown in the equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy}$. Thus, $RQ = \frac{6CO_2}{6O_2} = 1.0$.

subsections.title: RQ for Fats
subsections.content_text: When fats are used in respiration, the RQ is less than 1. For a fatty acid like tripalmitin, the calculation is: $2(C_{51}H_{98}O_6) + 145O_2 \rightarrow 102CO_2 + 98H_2O + \text{energy}$. Here, $RQ = \frac{102CO_2}{145O_2} = 0.7$.

subsections.title: RQ for Proteins and Mixed Substrates
subsections.content_text: When proteins are respiratory substrates, the ratio is about 0.9. In living organisms, respiratory substrates are often more than one; pure proteins or fats are never used as respiratory substrates.

subsections.title: Chapter Summary Part 1
subsections.content_text: Plants unlike animals have no special systems for breathing or gaseous exchange. Stomata and lenticels allow gaseous exchange by diffusion. Almost all living cells in a plant have their surfaces exposed to air. The breaking of C-C bonds of complex organic molecules by oxidation leading to the release of a lot of energy is called cellular respiration. Glucose is the favoured substrate for respiration. Fats and proteins can also be broken down to yield energy. The initial stage of cellular respiration takes place in the cytoplasm. Each glucose molecule is broken through a series of enzyme catalysed reactions into two molecules of pyruvic acid. This process is called glycolysis. The fate of the pyruvate depends on the availability of oxygen and the organism. Under anaerobic conditions either lactic acid fermentation or alcohol fermentation occurs. Fermentation takes place under anaerobic conditions in many prokaryotes, unicellular eukaryotes and in germinating seeds. In eukaryotic organisms aerobic respiration occurs in the presence of oxygen. Pyruvic acid is transported into the mitochondria where it is converted into acetyl CoA with the release of $CO_2$. Acetyl CoA then enters the tricarboxylic acid pathway or Krebs' cycle operating in the matrix of the mitochondria. $NADH + H^+$ and $FADH_2$ are generated in the Krebs' cycle. The energy in these molecules as well as that in the $NADH + H^+$ synthesised during glycolysis are used to synthesise ATP. This is accomplished through a system of electron carriers called electron transport system (ETS) located on the inner membrane of the mitochondria. The electrons, as they move through the system, release enough energy that are trapped to synthesise ATP. This is called oxidative phosphorylation. In this process $O_2$ is the ultimate acceptor of electrons and it gets reduced to water. The respiratory pathway is an amphibolic pathway as it involves both anabolism and catabolism. The respiratory quotient depends upon the type of respiratory substance used during respiration.

items.problem: Differentiate between (a) Respiration and Combustion (b) Glycolysis and Krebs’ cycle (c) Aerobic respiration and Fermentation
What are respiratory substrates? Name the most common respiratory substrate.
Give the schematic representation of glycolysis?
What are the main steps in aerobic respiration? Where does it take place?
Give the schematic representation of an overall view of Krebs’ cycle.
Distinguish between the following: (a) Aerobic respiration and Anaerobic respiration (b) Glycolysis and Fermentation (c) Glycolysis and Citric acid Cycle
What are the assumptions made during the calculation of net gain of ATP?
Discuss “The respiratory pathway is an amphibolic pathway.”
Define RQ. What is its value for fats?
What is the significance of step-wise release of energy in respiration?
